coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly flexographic ink from fluorene-based Schiff base pigment for anti-counterfeiting and printed electronics applications
coredata.dc:creator: Muthamma, Kashmitha
Sunil, Dhanya
Shetty, Prakasha
Kulkarni, Suresh D.
Anand, P.J.
Kekuda, Dhananjaya
coredata.dc:description: The challenge to prevent the counterfeiting of documents and the development of flexible printed electronics are two thrust research areas that demand innovative techniques. This study reports the use of fluorene-based Schiff base (FBH) prepared via a simple, one-step and cost-effective method as a thermally stable and biocompatible pigment for environment-friendly, water-based flexographic ink for potential application in security printing and flexible printed electronics. The flexo ink coating and print on the UV dull security paper were invisible in the daylight but displayed bluish-green fluorescence under UV light. Moreover, the emission colour of the print was sensitive to changing pH, which further imparted the eco-friendly ink formulation an excellent covert performance that could be exploited for anti-counterfeit application. The functional ink formulation can also be smartly-utilized in stamp/ink pads to obtain invisible fingerprint for detecting forgery. The colorimetric, and densitometry studies, abrasion resistance and surface morphology analysis of both the coated and printed paper samples were also investigated. The electrical measurements of the prints on flexible and low-cost paper substrates with good mechanical properties exhibited high charge carrier mobility with p-type semiconductivity for use in organic printed transistors. This new functional ink could thus be a promising candidate for anti-counterfeiting applications, including food packaging, documents, labels and ink pads, as well as in secure printed electronics.

row.entry: Non-film forming resin emulsion offers good adhesion, and excellent film integrity with high slide angle, gloss and transparency
Film-forming resin emulsion provides hard film, fast-drying, gloss and transparency.
Improves the surface-slip, rub and scuff resistance properties of the printed ink on the substrate
Prevents the foaming of ink that gives rise to printing defects such as pinholes, fish eyes, etc
Reduces the surface tension in water-based systems and improves substrate wetting
Fast-acting solvent with high boiling point and low vapour pressure as thinner to adjust the viscosity of the ink
Stabilises the acidic acrylic emulsions in water-based systems by charge repulsion and controls pH

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly flexographic ink from fluorene-based Schiff base pigment for anti-counterfeiting and printed electronics applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The challenge to prevent the counterfeiting of documents and the development of flexible printed electronics are two thrust research areas that demand innovative techniques. This study reports the use of fluorene-based Schiff base (FBH) prepared via a simple, one-step and cost-effective method as a thermally stable and biocompatible pigment for environment-friendly, water-based flexographic ink for potential application in security printing and flexible printed electronics. The flexo ink coating and print on the UV dull security paper were invisible in the daylight but displayed bluish-green fluorescence under UV light. Moreover, the emission colour of the print was sensitive to changing pH, which further imparted the eco-friendly ink formulation an excellent covert performance that could be exploited for anti-counterfeit application. The functional ink formulation can also be smartly-utilized in stamp/ink pads to obtain invisible fingerprint for detecting forgery. The colorimetric, and densitometry studies, abrasion resistance and surface morphology analysis of both the coated and printed paper samples were also investigated. The electrical measurements of the prints on flexible and low-cost paper substrates with good mechanical properties exhibited high charge carrier mobility with p-type semiconductivity for use in organic printed transistors. This new functional ink could thus be a promising candidate for anti-counterfeiting applications, including food packaging, documents, labels and ink pads, as well as in secure printed electronics.

ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeiting or forging of documents, banknotes, high-value merchandise, medicines, daily goods etc. is a major global issue that not only poses a threat to the individual and society, but also causes substantial economic impact [1–4]. Therefore, it is of explicit and utmost importance to prevent such illegal activities to safeguard these documents and prevent economic loss. However, the advancement in technology and predictability of the current techniques support the counterfeiters to forge the material easily. Hence, there is a grave need to develop unique anti-counterfeiting strategies that are easier to authenticate, but harder to copy [5,6]. Recently, different types of luminescent materials such as lanthanides [7], conventional organic materials/dyes/pigments [8], plasmonic nanomaterials [9], quantum dots [10], metal-organic-frameworks [11] and carbon-dots [12] are being explored as fluorescent/phosphorescent pigments for the fabrication of security inks and coated papers in the field of anti-counterfeits. But most of these materials have associated limitations; predominantly time-consuming multi-step synthesis protocols, high cost of raw materials, low photo/thermal stability, and high toxicity [13–18]. Therefore, it is necessary to intelligently design a luminescent material/fluorophore with unique optical, thermal and electrical properties by incorporating suitable functional groups. Nevertheless, despite numerous reports on luminophores, eco-friendly methods for fabricating biocompatible inks with covert security features are scarce [19].

In security printing for anti-counterfeit applications, in addition to the luminophoric pigment, the ink formulation, the substrate chosen and the print properties are also crucial. Nowadays, for eco-friendly motives, the printing industry focuses on producing water-reducible and water-based printing inks to minimise the release of harmful volatile organic compounds (VOCs) into the environment. Flexography is a high speed roll to roll printing method that can be applied on paper substrates with a print resolution of 5–100 mm for security and printed electronic applications [20]. The printing speed and the contact between impression roller and substrate is crucial for the flexo printing capability and yield [21]. Water-based flexographic inks are not only environment friendly, but are also capable of producing good quality prints on paper substrates (both uncoated and coated) and non-porous substrates, including metallised plastic films used in the packaging materials, and is the simplest of the printing processes [18,22]. The water-based flexo inks printed on flexible, low-cost and recyclable paper-based substrates find their use in ‘throw-away printed electronics’ too. Printed electronics own several unique advantages such as eco-friendly technique with minimum waste generation, lesser time from design to device manufacture, use of flexible substrates, reduced cost etc. The fast-evolving area of printed electronics using organic semiconducting materials demand for low cost, large area, flexible and lightweight devices, especially printed sensors, foldable electrochromic displays, capacitors, field effect transistors, RFID antennas, photovoltaic cells and resistors [23–27].

There are no literature evidences on the use of fluorene containing fluorescent pigments in the preparation of water-based flexo inks. With the above background highlighting the need for new functional materials that own attractive features for practical applications, we report the simple synthesis and structural characterization of a fluorene-based Schiff base FBH. The luminescent molecule obtained in good yield through a single step, cost-effective protocol is explored for its efficacy to act as a fluorophoric pigment for water-dispersed flexographic ink formulation with reliable and durable features. The print properties and security features (concealing performance and real-world usability) on paper substrates are investigated for anti-counterfeiting and flexible electronics applications.

The computational simulations and Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were executed using the B3LYP functions of Schrödinger's Materials Science Suite with a 6-31G++ basis set in vacuum. The stable ground state of FBH was optimised at B3LYP functional and the 3D model builder was used to design the electronic distribution in HOMO and LUMO levels. The solvents and chemicals used for the synthesis and characterization of FBH pigment were acquired from Sigma, Finar, and Spectrochem Chemicals Pvt. Ltd.

((9H-Fluoren-2-yl)methylene)-2-hydroxybenzohydrazide (FBH): An ethanolic solution of salicyloylhydrazide (0.5 mmol) was added to fluorene-2-carboxaldehyde (0.5 mmol) dissolved in ethanol and was refluxed at 80 °C for 6 h. The precipitate obtained was filtered, washed with ethanol and dried. The product was recrystallised from ethanol to obtain FBH as off-white crystals. The synthetic pathway for convenient preparation of FBH is presented in Scheme 1 .

The open capillary technique was followed to determine the melting point of FBH. The FT-IR and NMR spectra (DMSO‑d 6 as the solvent and TMS as the internal standard) were recorded using Shimadzu – IR Spirit analyzer and 400 MHz Bruker spectrophotometer. The mass spectrum was obtained using Agilent 6430 Triple Quad with electrospray ionisation. The electronic spectra were recorded using 1800 Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer and the emission spectra in JASCO spectrofluorometer FP 8300. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using HITACHI-Simultaneous Thermogravimetric Analyzer STA7000 series at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under nitrogen atmosphere. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique was used to measure the particle size of the FBH aggregates using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument. The effects of pH on the fluorescence profile of FBH was studied using 1 × 10−4 M FBH stock solution in DMF. The different pH of the FBH solution was maintained using 1 N H2SO4, 1 N NaOH, and their dilute solutions. The pH measurements were obtained using the Digital pH-meter MK VI equipped with a calibrated combined glass electrode.

The ink preparation involved two steps: (i) preparation of the ink concentrate with FBH as pigment and (ii) later obtaining the final ink formulation using the ink concentrate by adding the required additives. Initially, FBH (10.54%), acrylic emulsion 1 (58.73%), wetting and dispersing agent (1.18%), and carbitol (29.55%) were thoroughly mixed and transferred to the Llyod's pigment muller 92N at 40 Kgf for complete dispersion and milling of components to obtain the ink concentrate/mill base. Secondly, after the grind check, the remaining ink components or additives were added into the mill base and mixed thoroughly. Table 1 depicts the percentage composition of various components in the water-based flexo ink formulation.

The particle size of FBH in the ink formulation was checked using Zehntner grindometer and the viscosity was determined using Zahn cup #3. The X- Ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis of the dried ink film layer was carried out using Rigaku Miniflex 600 with CuKα radiation of wavelength 1.54 Å and 2θ range from 5° to 80° at a scan rate of 4° per minute. The UV dull paper obtained from Manipal Press, Pvt Ltd, Manipal, India, was used as the coating/printing substrate. The grammage (in gsm) and thickness of the paper substrate was tested using Digital GSM tester and Mitutoyo thickness gauge, respectively. The coating of the flexo ink on the UV dull paper was achieved using K bar coater (Bar Number = 0, wet film deposit = 4 μm). The printing of paper substrates (105 × 297 mm dimensions) was performed using RK Flexiproof 100 at 50 m/min with Anilox roll of 9.5 BCM, chambered doctor blade inking system and photopolymer plate. After printing, the substrates were dried at room temperature, and the samples used for further analysis. The density, L*, a*, b* and ΔE values were measured using Xrite I1 Pro spectrophotometer. The colour stability of the printed samples was checked using Hari Impex LAB UV 83002 instrument. The abrasion resistance of the ink on the printed substrates was verified using a Sutherland ink rub tester with 2 lbs. weight for 50 rubs. The surface morphological features of the coated and printed paper substrates were analysed using Carl Zeiss EVO 18 analytical scanning electron microscope (SEM) and INNOVA SPM atomic force microscope (AFM). The mechanical properties such as folding endurance and bursting strength were determined using MIT-type folding endurance tester and Bursting strength tester (Test Techno Consultants, model 3125 AM), respectively. Hall Effect experiment was performed at room temperature using Keithley Hall Effect set up at 3706 A.

In order to test the biocompatibility of FBH, MTT assay was performed in Vero cells. About 5 × 103 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C in CO2 for 24 h. The cells were treated with FBH at various concentrations of 0.5–3 μM and were further incubated for 24 h. Media was discarded, the cells were treated with MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) reagent (1 mg/mL) and incubated for another 4 h at 37 °C. DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan crystals formed, and the absorbance was recorded at 405 nm using a Varioskan flash plate reader (Thermo Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). The percentage viability of cells was calculated by the following formula:
% Cell viability =
Test absorbance − Blank absorbance
Vehicle control absorbance − Blank absorbance
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and DFT computational studies of FBH
ce:section.ce:para: Schiff bases are condensation products of aldehydes and amines, and carry an azomethine group RCNR′, (R and R′ are alkyl, cycloalkyl, aryl or heterocyclic group) in their structural framework. They are most widely used as pigments and dyes [28–31]. The lone pair of electrons in the sp2 hybridised orbital on the imino nitrogen atom makes the azomethine group chemically and biologically active [32]. Moreover, aromatic azomethine compounds that incorporate substituents with push-pull effect can interact with acids, bases, and polar solvent molecules because of the presence of basic and acidic sites and the delocalised π-electron systems in their chemical structure. Fluorene is an ortho-fused tricyclic mildly acidic non-polar arene and is a precursor to many commercially useful derivatives. These derivatives are generally blue-emitting materials exhibiting great flexibility in tuning their emission colour [33–36]. Meanwhile, Salicyloyl hydrazide derivatives have garnered wide attention as convenient fluorophores for various analytical and biological applications [37–39]. Hence, in this study, a Schiff's base FBH was designed (Fig. 1a) with a fluorene moiety linked to salicyloyl hydrazide through an azomethine spacer with the aim of achieving a semiconducting fluorophoric functional pigment for the preparation of an environmental-friendly flexo-ink formulation that can offer high level security features for anti-counterfeit application and printed electronics [40].

The DFT simulations were performed to generate the optimised molecular geometry of the designed FBH molecule (Fig. 1b) and to comprehend the electron density distributions in the molecular orbital energy levels [41]. The HOMO and LUMO energy values of FBH were found to be −5.3916 and −1.5045 eV, respectively with an energy bandgap of 3.8871 eV. It is well-known that intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) significantly affects the emission properties of some chromophoric molecules containing donor (D) and acceptor (A) groups [42,43]. From the HOMO levels of FBH shown in Fig. 1c, the electron density is seen to be significantly delocalised on the fluorene fragment and the azomethine spacer of the donor-bridge-acceptor (D-π-A) system, whereas the electron density of the LUMO as observed from Fig. 1d is mainly populated on the entire molecule. The electron distribution in the molecular orbitals suggests a possible ICT phenomenon, sequentially from the donor part to the imine linker and then from the linker unit to the acceptor within the FBH molecule [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization and intrinsic features of FBH pigment
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequent to theoretical studies, FBH was synthesised in good yield using a simple, one-step protocol. The chemical characterization data of the Schiff's base is provided below, which is in agreement with the designed structure of FBH.

Further, the unique features of FBH, such as aggregate forming tendency, ICT process, colour stability, thermal stability, and pH responsive fluorescence that could be conveniently exploited to use the molecule as a functional pigment in eco-friendly water-based ink formulation were investigated. As FBH displayed maximum violet colored emission (λ
em = 431 nm) in THF (Fig. S6) at an excitation wavelength of 367 nm, further solution-phase analysis was carried out in 1 × 10−4 M THF solution of FBH at λ
ex = 367 nm. Interestingly, solid FBH showed a red-shifted fluorescence emission at 489 nm (Fig. S7) in the blue region, which indicated its dual state emission feature.

Aggregation induced emission (AIE) is a phenomenon widely used in various applications such as biosensing, bioimaging, luminescence devices, etc. There are several literature evidences available on the AIE property exhibited by various Schiff base derivatives [45–48]. Hence, as dual state emission was perceived, the AIE property of FBH was examined in THF-water mixtures with increasing water fractions. The emission characteristics of various 1 × 10−5 M THF-water fractions (0 to 99% water) are depicted in Fig. 2a. The fluorescence intensity (λ
em
= 408 nm) gradually decreased with rise in water fractions (0 to 70%). Upon further increase in water fraction (80%), a new red shifted emission was observed at 481 nm. The emission intensity increased until the water percentage reached 90, and later reduced when the amount of water approached 99%. This interesting phenomenon could be attributed to either the formation of aggregates, or enhanced hydrogen bonding with increased water fraction; the dissolved molecules combine to form two different states: crystalline aggregates and amorphous particles [49,50]. Hence, the formation of aggregates was further explored using DLS measurements of 1 × 10−5 M solution of FBH in THF:water (10:90) mixture. The 90% water fraction displayed a particle size of 72.9 nm as presented in Fig. S8a, which indicates the aggregate formation of FBH. The zeta potential value of −10.7 mV (Fig. S8b) showed that the particles can experience agglomeration in THF solution of FBH with 90% water fraction. Further, the surface morphology (Fig. 2b and c) of the 0% and 90% water fractions of 1 × 10−5 M THF-water mixtures of FBH was analysed, and the obtained SEM image of 90% water fraction exhibited fibre like aggregation as depicted in Fig. 2c.

As the DFT simulations suggested the occurrence of ICT, the emission characteristics of FBH was investigated in 1 × 10−5 M THF/hexane mixtures. The phenomenon of ICT can be observed in polar solvents and usually results in a hypsochromically-shifted emission colour with decreasing solvent polarity [51]. The involvement of an ICT process in the molecule was evident from Fig. 2d as a blue-shift in the emission from ~433 to 425 nm occurred upon addition of small amounts of non-polar hexane.

Colour stability is the visual property of a pigment that defines its resistance to fading when exposed to light, and is important in the commercial application of an ink formulation. The colour fading studies were carried out for powdered FBH sample (35 mg) by spreading it evenly throughout a 4 × 4 cm paper and exposing under sunlight for 30 days and under 365 nm UV light for various durations (0–4 h). A pale-yellow coloration for FBH was perceived on exposure to sunlight only after 30 days. Fig. S9 depicts the photographs of FBH samples observed under daylight and UV light for the specified UV exposure durations. There were no significant colour variations of solid FBH samples when viewed in daylight, and also in the fluorescence emission colors after UV exposure. The fluorescence spectra of all these samples, as presented in Fig. 2e, exhibited a bluish-green emission at around 489 nm. A small increase in emission intensity was observed for the FBH sample on 1 h exposure to UV light compared to the unexposed FBH. Upon subsequent UV exposure for 2 h, a slight reduction in fluorescence intensity was perceived. Interestingly, there was no significant reduction in emission intensity on further exposures for 3 and 4 h, compared to unexposed FBH. Further, the CIE plots (Fig. 2f) were observed to evaluate the pigment's emission colour characteristics upon UV exposure. (0.2287, 0.3388), (0.2303, 0.3444), (0.237, 0.353) and (0.2391, 0.3558) were the chromaticity coordinates (x,y) values of FBH at 0, 1, 2, 3 and 4 h, respectively, which indicated that there is no significant change in the fluorescence emission colour and colour strength even after prolonged exposure of 4 h.

UV rays are energetic enough to break down certain bonds in a pigmented ink system and thus modify its molecular structure, resulting in an irreversible loss of colour strength or variation in hue [52]. In Schiff bases, UV light can trigger isomerisation [30]. Therefore, to check the possibilities of isomerisation of FBH on exposure to UV radiation, the absorption spectrum of the above UV-exposed/unexposed FBH samples were recorded and compared. It was observed from the electronic plots presented in Fig. 2g, that only the intensity of absorption at 341.6 nm for FBH increased upon exposure to UV light for 1 and 2 h, whereas for both the samples after 3 and 4 h exposure respectively, the absorptions are similar as in the case of unexposed FBH. Therefore, there is no possible CN isomerisation upon UV-light exposure, and this could be due to the restriction of intramolecular rotations (RIR) of the molecules in the solid state [53,54].

Temperature-induced decomposition of pigments reduces its commercial utility. TGA analysis plays a vital role in understanding phase transitions and gives insights into a fluorophore's thermal stability. The TGA plots of weight percentage against temperature as portrayed in Fig. S10 provides clear evidence for the thermal stability of FBH up to 280 °C. The TGA curves of FBH indicated two weight loss steps; the first step observed around 300 °C with a loss of 39.8% weight could be due to the disintegration of organic intermediates, and the second step at 600 °C with a weight loss of ~60% might be due to the gradual degradation of FBH framework. It is observed that there is no weight loss after 700 °C, which infers that the organic component (FBH) is completely removed [55,56].

Stimuli-responsive fluorophores are highly beneficial for security applications, and many Schiff bases have been used as pH sensors [57]. The intramolecular hydrogen bonding between the phenolic-OH and imine N in Schiff bases is generally susceptible to the acidic condition. The OH…N hydrogen bond is disrupted due to the protonation of imine nitrogen. The nitrogen of the azomethine group has a basic character due to the lone pair explicitly present on the atom, and can hence take up a proton to form the conjugate acid of the Schiff base, or the protonated Schiff base [58]. In addition to the azomethine unit, FBH incorporates OH and NH groups in its structural framework, which can be deprotonated and protonated in presence of bases and acids in the surrounding environment. Moreover, the electron-withdrawing carbonyl functional group can generate an acidic NH proton in its vicinity, which subsequently can create a conjugate base or anion, with an electron-donating ability. This anion can act as an electron-donating moiety in conjugation with the electron-deficient fluorene moiety leading to an interesting donor-acceptor molecular system under pH stimulus.

Due to the above protonation-deprotonation possibilities of FBH, the fluorescence responses of the Schiff base on exposure to varying pH were investigated by adding H2SO4/NaOH. The colourless solution of FBH undergoes a visible colour change to intense yellow when the pH approaches 10 as presented in the photograph in Fig. 3a. The solution reverts to colourless on further decreasing the pH, and this process was reversible. When visually observed under the UV lamp source of 365 nm, the acidic solutions of FBH emitted blue (pH = 2 and 4) and bluish green (pH = 6 and 8), but a visible yellow fluorescence is perceived as the pH increases to 10. To confirm these interesting pH-sensitive optical changes, the fluorescence spectra of these solutions were recorded and is depicted in Fig. 3b. As the pH varies from 2 to 8, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity changed from bright blue fluorescence to light blue fluorescence. The decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing pH indicates the reduced degree of protonation. The increasing negative charge density further affects the energy transfer, eventually leading to fluorescence change [59]. An appreciable hypsochromic shift in emission wavelength from 475 nm to 431 nm was observed when the pH rises to 10 (Fig. 3b). The CIE plots of these FBH solutions presented in Fig. 3c with (0.1890, 0.2496) and (0.1980, 0.1397) respectively as CIE coordinates for FBH solutions of pH 2 and 10 also supports the above observations. The pH sensory displayed by FBH is attributed to the modulating ICT through deprotonation/protonation process. The pH triggered colour change under visible light and fluorescence emission changes under UV-light of FBH that could be perceived using naked eyes could be an efficient way to bring in semi-covert security features, while using its ink formulations [57].

The demand for water-based environment-friendly alternatives for flexographic inks is gravely escalating, and is reinforced by the need for alternative solutions to address certain vital public issues such as environment polluting emissions from VOCs that impact user health, global carbon dioxide emissions, toxicity, and the energy consumed in printing. The new emulsions (resin/water suspensions) enable the flexo ink formulations to be water-based, thereby limiting the glitches of solvent-based formulations. Therefore, in this study, water-based flexographic printing ink was prepared using FBH as the pigment. The preparation of the ink concentrate is the first step, wherein proper mixing with the ink components and uniform dispersion of FBH is crucial for the ink to perform well on press and on the substrate. The FBH agglomerates were broken down into individual crystal size through the milling process and mixed with the components to obtain a homogenous blend by removing the air or water on the crystal surface. The final water-based ink formulation for flexographic printing with pH = 7–8 was obtained by adding other varnishes and additives. The formulated ink was white-colored and was fluorescent when viewed under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The fineness-of-grind or the particle size of the final ink formulation, tested using the grindometer was found to be less than 10 μm. The viscosity of the as-prepared water-based flexo-ink was found to be 0.175 Pa.s at room temperature. Further, the ink was coated on a glass slide and subjected to SEM analysis. Fig. S11a exhibited a well-dispersed ink film without any agglomerates. A close visualisation of the SEM image in Fig. S11b displayed the particle size to be less than 2 μm. Subsequently, the XRD analysis of the dried ink film layer was carried out to probe the nature of the ink. The XRD plots, as shown in Fig. S11c exhibited crystalline nature of the particles in the ink formulation similar to that of FBH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of flexographic ink coating/print properties on UV dull paper substrates
ce:section.ce:para: As per the ISO 12647-6 standard, the substrates used for flexographic printing must have ‘L*’ value ≥ 90, ‘a*’ value ≤ 3 and ‘b*’ value ≤ 5. The UV dull papers of 80 gsm and 0.121 mm thickness with L*, a*, b* and density values of 95, 0.5, 4.2 and 0.1 respectively, used in the present study as flexographic coating and printing substrates fulfilled the criteria of ISO 12647-6. The water-based ink was initially coated on the paper substrate by the drawdown method. The preliminary visual analysis was carried out to determine the accuracy of colour matching and mixing processes, the compatibility of various ink components, the performance of the ink on the substrate and the drying characteristics of the ink. After coating, the paper sample dried quickly at room temperature.

Fascinatingly, the coating of flexo-ink on the security paper as shown in Fig. 4a was invisible in daylight. However, when viewed under 365 nm UV light, a bright bluish-green fluorescence was observed, as portrayed in Fig. 4b. Further, to explore the coating thickness on fluorescence properties, a drawdown of the ink was carried out to obtain three layers (C 1–C 3). After visual observation; invisible under daylight (Fig. 4c) and bluish-green fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 4d), the samples were subjected to fluorescence measurements (λ
ex = 370 nm). It is observed from Fig. 4e that the thickness of the ink film did not have much effect on its emission characteristics. A single layer coating displayed higher fluorescence intensity and, it was concluded that there is no need for a double hit during the printing process. The CIE plots (Fig. 4f) for these samples were recorded to evaluate the ink performance for luminescence by comparing fluorescence emission colour with the colour coordinates. The CIE chromaticity coordinates (x,y) values for C 1, C 2 and C 3 were found to be (0.2192, 0.3051), (0.2161, 0.2996) and (0.2217, 0.3134), respectively, which were suggestive of only minimal variations in fluorescence colour with coating thickness.

Based on the observations on the coating studies of the ink formulation, a solid patch was printed on the UV dull paper of 105 mm × 297 mm dimension using the flexography process. The dried ink film was 6 μm thick, and the print was invisible to the naked eye. The fluorescence spectra recorded for the printed paper (P 1) sample (Fig. 4g) showed slightly lesser fluorescence intensity than the coated one, which could be due to the parameters like pressure and speed that are uncontrollable when the coating was done manually. The CIE chromaticity coordinates of C 1 (0.2192, 0.3051) and for P 1 (printed - 1 hit) (0.2049, 0.2567) were obtained from the CIE plots presented in Fig. 4h.

The photostability of the ink film was tested in UV curing equipment by exposing an evenly cut 1.5 × 1.5 cm coated (C 1) and printed (P 1) samples to UV light of 300 W/30 m/min for 30 (C/P-30), 60 (C/P-60) and 90 (C/P-90) cycles. The emission spectra recorded for these samples are depicted in Fig. 5a for the coated and Fig. 5c for the printed samples for comparison with that of UV-unexposed coated/printed samples. The emission intensities were more for 30 pass UV exposed coated/printed samples, which decreased steadily and almost decreased to the level of the unexposed sample after 90 passes. The CIE plots as shown in Fig. 5b and d for these samples were recorded to evaluate the ink performance for luminescence colour, which suggested a slight red shift on exposure to UV light, compared to the unexposed sample. Fig. 5e portrays the images of the printed samples under daylight and for the samples before and after exposure to 365 nm UV light.

International Commission on Illumination has defined three dimensional CIELAB colour spaces (designed to approximate human vision), and these were used to monitor the colour changes or chromaticity parameters of the coated and printed samples. The CIE has recommended L*, a* and b* for colour description. The L* value ranges from 0 to 100 (0 indicates black; 100 indicates white), closely matching the human perception of lightness/luminance component in an image and helps predict minor colour differences. The L* value decreases as yellowing occurs. The two chromatic components, a* (from green to red) and b* (from blue to yellow), range from −120 to 120. Negative a* values indicate green, and positive values indicate red, whereas negative b* values indicate blue and positive values yellow. If ΔE value is 0–2 then it is invisible; if 2–3 experts can recognise, and any individual can recognise if ΔE > 3 [60,61].

The colorimetric and densitometric values for the coated and printed samples are presented in Supplementary Table 1 (Table S1). The data shows that the L* value for the blank UV dull paper substrate is 96.2 which is close to 100, which indicates that the colour observed is white. The L* values of C 1 (95.5) and P 1 (95.4) are very close to the blank paper, indicating that the coat or print is invisible. The positive b* values indicated yellowness, i.e., upon increase in the number of coating and upon increase in number of exposure cycles, the ink film appears yellow. The ΔE = 2 suggest that both the coated and printed samples are invisible to naked eye. The graphical representation of the L*, a*, b*, ΔE and density values for colour description are presented in Fig. 6a–e.

The abrasion resistance defines the degree to which the original print remains unaltered upon repeated rubbing and scuffing. The abrasion resistance test was designed to simulate the form of damage against a print that may result from paper rubbing. To determine the ability to withstand the effects of rubbing and scuffing, a strip of 8 × 5 cm dimension of the printed sample and the unprinted sample were fixed on the rubber pad and one on the base plate respectively, and a weight of 2 lbs. was placed over the samples with the test block rubber pads over the ink. The tester was set for 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 100 strokes, and after each set of strokes, the colour parameters and optical density values were recorded. Table S2 depicts the colorimetric and densitometry values for the rub tested samples. The negligible change in the values from 0 to 100 rubs suggests the printed ink film's stability and high abrasion resistance. The plot of colorimetric values of the printed sample after rub test presented in Fig. 6f gives a clear indication of the binding strength of the ink film on the paper substrate.

As the flexo-prints with FBH pigment displayed strong and stable fluorescence and good transparency in the visible region, the water-based formulation can be a suitable candidate for fluorescent security ink. To explore the applicability, a text was written using the ink formulation after three months of its preparation, on UV dull security paper. As shown in Fig. 7a, the paper appeared blank under daylight, but the writing ‘FBH’ alone was clearly visible with bluish green fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 7b). The stability of the ink was evident from this experiment and moreover, the text on the paper remained consistent with reproducible results even after 30 days when stored under ambient conditions. These observations propose that the FBH formulation can be utilized as invisible ink to encrypt secret information/communications and have a significant prospective for anti-counterfeiting applications [62].

Although many fluorescent materials have been implemented widely to protect government documents, high-value merchandise and banknotes, they can be mimicked rather easily. Therefore, inks with more complex security features can present a formidable challenge to the counterfeiter. The unique photophysical properties of FBH under acidic and basic conditions prompted us to examine the effects of pH on the emission of the printed paper samples [63,64]. The printed samples were cut into 1.5 × 1.5 cm, and exposed to 0.5 N H2SO4 and 0.5 N NaOH and the photographs of these samples under daylight and UV light are presented in Fig. 7c. Under daylight, there was no visible colour change observed, and the print was invisible at all studied pH. However, under 365 nm UV light, the untreated and acid treated prints showed blue fluorescence. But, yellow fluorescence emission was observed from NaOH treated print. Moreover, to check the reversibility of pH-sensitive emission behavior of flexo-prints from FBH, the yellow emitting sample was further exposed to H2SO4 solution, which reverted back to exhibit blue fluorescence. Thus, the bluish-green fluorescence of the prints and texts can be altered by spraying NaOH to observe yellow fluorescence under UV light source upon visual examination, which can be switched back to blue emission on further addition of H2SO4, due to deprotonation-protonation of the functional units present in FBH. To analyse these emission changes experimentally, the fluorescence spectra of all the samples were recorded and is presented in Fig. S12a. No significant change in emission colour or intensity was observed in the FBH print exposed to acid. This was further confirmed by the respective CIE plot (Fig. S12b) with values of (0.1991, 0.2409) and (0.2023, 0.2498) for the untreated and acid-treated prints, respectively. The print exposed to NaOH resulted in an appreciable shift in the fluorescence intensity and CIE colour coordinates. Further addition of acid shifted back the emission colour to that of the untreated printed sample. Thus, the molecular structure of FBH allows a pH sensitive authentication mechanism. The information within a pre-existing image printed using the ink formulation can be verified by simply spraying or wiping an authentication indicator (acid or base) over the printed image. This pH sensitive behavior can add more covert security feature for secret data encryption, which will be difficult to replicate, and can be used in advanced anti-counterfeiting.

Human fingerprints are often placed on important documents. Hence, to further explore the potential use of FBH flexo-ink in ink pads, the inherent cytotoxicity profile of FBH was tested using MTT assay. The pigment did not show any significant cell death in Vero cells up to 3 μM concentration. The MTT assay plot on concentration dependent cell viability as presented in Fig. S12c thus denoted the excellent biocompatibility of FBH, which is a key requirement. Fig. 7d depicts the invisible fingerprint using FBH-ink on security paper in daylight. Interestingly, the formulated ink showcases a clear, long-lasting, and bluish-green fluorescent fingerprint when viewed under UV light (Fig. 7e). The ink can be easily cleaned with water without contaminating the fingers demonstrating their versatility [62,65,66]. Moreover, the dried fingerprint on the paper could not be washed away by water, which might be due to the interaction between the OH and NH groups present on FBH with the cellulose fibres of the paper, thereby preventing the dispersion of ink by the water molecules [62]. This clearly validates the safe use of the ink formulation in ink pads to form human fingerprints that could serve as an alternative to the traditional inks. Moreover, the functional ink formulation can be smartly-utilized in stamp/ink pads to obtain invisible fingerprint for detecting forgery. The invisible fluorescent ink can also be used in fingerprint detection for forensic investigation of documents, and in bank notes, fluorescent tags, watermarks, holograms, glossy strips etc., as they are not visible in plain sunlight, but glow under an UV light source, and thereby aid in tackling counterfeiting.

Despite a long history of paper manufacturing and printing, the research advancements in these regimes started to evolve recently with new value-added products that showcase novel functionalities. Paper-based substrates are utilized in various printed applications such as sensors, displays, etc. apart from the conventional industry [67–70]. Throw-away electronics printed using conducting and semiconducting functional inks with large area on paper-based substrates could be produced in bulk with their improvised use in everyday applications [71–76]. For these organic printed products to come into regular use, devices with good electrical performance and lower manufacturing cost are required [77,78]. While using paper as a substrate for printed electronics, there are evident benefits including low cost, biodegradability, flexibility and fibre recycling or ease of disposal through incineration. Moreover, the eco-friendly water-based flexographic ink possesses attractive features such as high-speed fabrication, low-cost manufacturing, the versatility of ink viscosities, less waste, roll-to-roll capability, and obtaining the desired printed pattern on flexible paper substrates, which holds distinct potential for use in printed electronics [79]. Therefore, in the present study, the flexo-prints obtained on UV dull paper substrate were investigated for their surface morphological and mechanical features and electrical properties for possible use as a functional ink in printed electronics.

The surfaces of the unprinted and printed paper samples were viewed using SEM, and AFM techniques and the respective microscopic images obtained are shown in Fig. 8 . The SEM images of the unprinted substrate displayed fibrous nature (Fig. 8a), whereas smoother surface was perceived for printed paper sample (Fig. 8b). The flexo-print produces a homogeneous and relatively regular ink film that fills the porous fibrous structure of the paper [80], improving the water vapour resistance. Moreover, the AFM images presented in Fig. 8c and d in combination with the average surface roughness (Ra), root mean square roughness (Rq) and maximum roughness depth (Rmax) values (Table S3) are suggestive of a smoother printed ink film on the paper sample. While fabricating the device, the surface roughness can be further reduced by using unique or engineered paper with inherently minor surface roughness or by applying a smoothening surface coating before device fabrication [81].

Flexibility refers to the ability of the printed material to deform and it can be demonstrated in terms of bending/folding, stretching or bursting [82]. The folding endurance was carried out to determine the ability of the printed substrate to withstand repeated bending, folding and creasing. Generally, the fold endurance on light-weight papers (70 double folds and under) is more when folded in cross-grain direction, whereas fold endurance on heavy-weight papers (100 double folds and higher) is more when scored and folded in grain direction [83]. About 0.5 × 6 in. samples were used for performing the folding endurance test at 0.5 kg load and with a speed of 60 double folds per minute. The number of double folds made before fracture in-grain direction and against-grain direction was 37 and 71, respectively, which suggested good folding endurance for the printed substrate. Bursting strength is widely used as a measure of resistance to rupture in various types of papers and boards. The bursting strength of single sheet of printed material was 1.6 kg/cm2, which indicated good mechanical property of the studied sample.

Hall effect measurements play a vital role in elucidating the fundamental charge transport mechanisms and intrinsic mobility for electronic device application. In this study, the conductivity properties of the flexo-ink printed on the UV dull paper substrate (P 1) was measured. The flexo-print with FBH as the pigment was found to be a p-type semiconductor. The print displayed a resistivity of 807.99 Ω cm, mobility of 98.17 cm2/Vs and a low carrier concentration of 7.87 × 1013 cm−3. All the studied electrical parameters are tabulated in Table S4, and these values suggest that the formulated water-based ink can be printed on suitable paper substrates for thin-film organic electrochemical transistor (OECT) applications. The simple fabrication process of OECT, low operating voltages, the flexible and eco-friendly paper substrate, the less sensitivity of the device performance to the substrate roughness, and large trans-conductance could make the OECT device prepared using the ink formulation via flexographic printing attractive. The devices developed by the formulated ink on paper substrates might also be particularly useful for applications in sensing, biological interfacing and thin film transistor [84,85]. The fabrication of these types of electronic devices on banknotes or confidential documents can be another avenue that can be further explored with possibilities of implementing active security and anti-counterfeiting features directly on their surface.

In summary, we have effectively demonstrated a simple, cost-effective and single-step synthesis of Schiff base FBH with good yield. The compound exhibited various attractive features such as AIE, ICT, pH sensitivity, good thermal and colour stability, and hence served as a promising functional pigment material for fabricating a water-based ink formulation. This low-cost, durable, environment-friendly and water-dispersible anti-counterfeiting ink was successfully coated and printed onto the surfaces of the UV dull paper by flexography-printing technique. The prints showed good adhesion, colour stability, abrasion resistance and pH-dependent fluorescence on paper substrates. Reversible colour changes in the fluorescence emission of the invisible print triggered by acid/base are a viable and semi-covert security feature for strategically building up fluorescent texts for information coding, encryption, and decryption, indicating their promising application in data security and high-level anti-counterfeiting field. The biocompatible and halochromic ink was further employed as an invisible ink for ink pads for fingerprint detection application to detect forgery as the invisible fingerprints displayed bluish-green fluorescence under a UV light source. Moreover, the electrical and mechanical measurements of the prints on paper substrates displayed its potential prospects in flexible optical/optoelectronic devices.
